case_name: Gysegem v. Ohio State Univ. Wexner Med. Ctr.

summary.brief: Gysegem v. Ohio State Univ. Wexner Med. Ctr.. Medical malpractice case involving paediatrics.

evidence_index.text: In February 2015 Tim Gysegem—who has been diagnosed with, among other things, monoclonal B cell lymphocytosis—presented to the emergency room at OSUWMC after he experienced abdominal pain and other symptoms, including fever and nausea.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: On February 23, 2015, a CT scan was performed on Tim Gysegem. A radiologist noted that the CT scan showed 'an extraluminal collection containing an air-fluid level adjacent to the appendix with an appendicolith in this region, measuring approximately 2.6 x 4.4 cm.'
evidence_index.speaker: Radiologist

evidence_index.text: On February 24, 2015, Dr. Eiferman performed a laparoscopic appendectomy on Tim Gysegem at OSUWMC.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr. Eiferman testified that he would have used a surgical instrument to 'get out what’s inside that abscess cavity, that pus, any stones, any inflammatory debris.'
evidence_index.speaker: Dr. Eiferman

evidence_index.text: Tim Gysegem was discharged from the hospital on February 26, 2015 with instructions to follow up with Dr. Eiferman.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: On March 3, 2015, Tim Gysegem underwent an endoscopic retrograde cholangiopancreatography (ERCP) with sphincterotomy to evaluate a potential biliary obstruction.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: On March 4, 2015, the interventional radiology team drained 10 ml of fluid, which was sent for culture.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: On March 9, 2015, Tim Gysegem was discharged from OSUWMC with instructions to schedule a follow-up appointment with Dr. Eiferman.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: On March 27, 2015, Dr. Eiferman performed a laparoscopic cholecystectomy on Tim Gysegem at OSUWMC.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Dr. Steinberg advised the Gysegems that the CT scan demonstrated an abscess in an area not previously seen and that it had encompassed the right lung, had gone through the diaphragm, and had invaded the chest.
evidence_index.speaker: Dr. Steinberg

evidence_index.text: The court concludes by preponderance of the evidence that Dr. Eiferman did not breach the standard of care during the laparoscopic appendectomy by failing to remove the appendicolith that was identified in the CT scan of February 23, 2015.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: The court finds by a preponderance of the evidence that Dr. Eiferman met the standard of care during the laparoscopic cholecystectomy.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: The Gysegems cannot prevail on their claim of medical negligence against OSUWMC.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

evidence_index.text: The court holds that the Gysegems have not proven by a preponderance of the evidence that OSUWMC should be held liable for medical malpractice or a derivative loss of consortium.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's conclusion

initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Patient presented to the emergency room with abdominal pain and other symptoms, including fever and nausea.

focused_exam.abdomen: Examination mentioned but findings not specified

critical_findings: Extraluminal collection with air-fluid level on CT

requestables.name: CT scan of abdomen
requestables.description: CT scan performed to evaluate abdominal pain

reveal.result_summary: Extraluminal collection with air-fluid level adjacent to the appendix with an appendicolith
reveal.clinical_significance: Consistent with a contained fluid collection secondary to perforated appendicitis

reveal.result_summary: Appendectomy performed, no mention of appendicolith removal
reveal.clinical_significance: Potential oversight in not documenting removal of appendicolith
reveal.decision_impact: Could lead to complications if appendicolith not removed

timeline_phases.name: Emergency Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Patient presents to ED with acute symptoms

decision_points.prompt: Should the appendicolith identified on CT be removed?

options.description: Ensure removal of appendicolith during surgery
options.clinical_reasoning: Standard practice to remove appendicolith to prevent infection
Risk of leaving appendicolith leading to complications

actual_action_defendant.reasoning_stated: Not explicitly stated in surgical note

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failure to ensure removal of appendicolith could lead to infection
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Appendicolith should have been removed during appendectomy
explanation.legal_standard_applied: Standard of care requires removal if safely possible

patient_outcome.description: Patient experienced recurring infections and required multiple follow-up procedures
patient_outcome.long_term_effects: Ongoing abdominal issues and multiple surgeries

key_findings: No breach of standard of care found
Plaintiffs failed to prove negligence by preponderance of evidence

treatments.outcome: Appendix removed, appendicolith removal not documented

court_findings.finding: No breach of standard of care
